turns.content: I keep thinking about persistent data structures, and it feels like a memory leak compared to how we handle immutable types in Rust with Arc or Rc. I know we can use AtomicRefCell for thread safety, but that still requires full copies of the data on every mutation, whereas functional approaches share structure instead.

You are right to compare this to Arc and Rsc, because both manage shared ownership, but persistent structures do something different: they avoid copying entirely by reusing existing nodes. Instead of creating a new heap-allocated struct for every change, these data structures use structural sharing so that only the changed part gets copied while the rest of the tree remains immutable and referenced by multiple versions. This means you can have a version history where each snapshot is just a pointer to the root node with O(1) access time, rather than iterating through all previous copies.

So if I want to implement this in Rust without using external crates like fxhash or persistent-hdbs, what should I focus on? Should I use Rc<RefCell> for the nodes and then manually implement the copy-on-write logic?

You are close, but you need to be careful about how you define the node structure. Instead of wrapping everything in RefCell, which introduces runtime overhead for interior mutability, consider using a tagged union or an enum where each variant holds either the actual data or a reference to another node. For example, a Node type could use Option<&T> to represent structural sharing, and when you need to update a value at a specific leaf, you only create a new branch for that path while keeping all other siblings pointing to the old nodes. This avoids unnecessary allocations because the unchanged parts are never copied.
I'm trying to implement a history tracker for my binary search tree but I keep hitting O(n) copy overhead on every update. How do I achieve O(1) snapshot creation in Rust?

The issue is you're copying the entire node structure instead of sharing it. In Rust, you need to use structural sharing where only changed nodes are cloned. This means your tree nodes shouldn't own their children; they should just reference them like a graph, so when you insert or delete, only the affected branch gets copied while the rest remains shared across versions.
So I can't use standard heap allocation for subtrees? What's the pattern?

You still allocate nodes on the heap, but you avoid deep copying. Think of it like immutable data structures where each mutation creates a new version by only duplicating the changed path. In Rust terms, your Node type should implement traits that allow partial cloning or you use smart pointers like Arc to share ownership safely between versions without manual dereferencing overhead.
I tried using Rc but it's not thread-safe enough for my concurrent history needs. What should I use instead?

Use Arc<T> or a thread-safe reference type like RefCell if you need interior mutability, but for true persistence where nodes are truly immutable after creation, standard Rust doesn't require special concurrency primitives because the data never changes. The key is ensuring your Node struct implements traits that allow efficient cloning of only modified fields while keeping unchanged subtrees shared via Arc or similar reference types.
Okay, so I'm building a persistent BST where each update creates a new root but shares all ancestors. But how do I actually implement the copy-on-write for branches without breaking memory safety?

The trick is to never mutate existing nodes once created. Every operation like insert or delete returns a brand new Node instance with only the changed subtree duplicated. Your implementation should look something like: fn update(node: &mut Option<Arc<Node>>, op: Op) -> Arc<Node> where you clone only the path from root to leaf and rebuild the rest by reusing existing Arc references. This way your history grows linearly in memory despite potentially millions of versions.
Got it, so I'm avoiding deep copy by sharing arcs through unchanged paths. One last thing though, how do I handle the O(1) snapshot requirement practically?

Since each new version is just a reference to an existing tree with one branch duplicated, taking a snapshot is trivial—you simply hold the root Arc and return it. No extra copying or serialization needed because the entire history is already represented as immutable references to previously constructed nodes. Your Rust code will naturally support this if you design your Node type to be clone-only-on-change rather than full-clone.
